prompt:
Postmortem action item from Tuesday's incident: the sync worker hammered the vendor API after a deploy and we ate a 429-storm for an hour. I'm assigning you the limiter. Build `ratelimit.py` with a classic token bucket:

`TokenBucket(capacity, refill_rate, clock)` — capacity is the burst size, refill_rate is tokens/second, and `clock` is any zero-arg callable returning seconds as a float (we pass time.monotonic in prod and a fake in tests — the whole point is that NOTHING in this module may call time.time/sleep itself). Bucket starts full. Refill is continuous, so fractions of a token accumulate between calls, capped at capacity.

Methods: `try_acquire(n=1)` returns True and spends n tokens, or returns False and spends absolutely nothing — no partial debits. `available` (property) reports the current balance without spending. `wait_time(n)` says how many seconds until n tokens will exist (0.0 if they already do) and must not spend either. Asking for n <= 0 or n > capacity is a ValueError (the latter would block forever — we want the loud failure). Constructor rejects capacity <= 0 or refill_rate <= 0 the same way.

One thing I insist on because it bit us with NTP steps on the VMs: if the clock ever goes BACKWARDS, the bucket must neither destroy tokens nor mint free ones when time catches back up. Track your high-water mark.

Also `KeyedLimiter(capacity, refill_rate, clock)` with `allow(key, n=1)` — lazily one bucket per key (per-tenant limiting), all sharing the clock and parameters.

Contract: `python3 test_ratelimit.py` green, tests untouched, stdlib only.
